questions.question: What is the main advantage of using the Temporal Docs MCP Server with an AI assistant?

choices.text: It gives the AI access to Temporal's source code so it can generate optimized builds automatically.
It connects the AI to current Temporal documentation in real time, so answers come from up-to-date docs rather than potentially outdated training data.
It replaces the need for Temporal SDKs by letting the AI generate workflows without any local installation.
It stores your workflow history in the cloud so the AI can replay and debug past executions on your behalf.

questions.explanation: AI models are trained on data up to a certain cutoff date, which means their knowledge of fast-moving tools like Temporal can become stale. The Temporal Docs MCP Server solves this by giving the AI real-time access to the actual documentation, so when you ask questions about Temporal, the answers are grounded in the current docs rather than whatever the model happened to learn during training. This is especially useful when APIs or best practices have changed since the model was trained.

questions.question: Temporal provides two different 'Skills' for AI coding agents — a Developer Skill and a Cloud Skill. Why would you install the Cloud Skill specifically?

choices.text: To help your AI agent troubleshoot issues related to Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration.
To enable your AI agent to deploy new Temporal Clusters automatically in any cloud provider account.
To give your AI agent the ability to write and execute Temporal Workflows directly inside cloud infrastructure.
To allow your AI agent to monitor Worker health metrics and autoscale Task Queues in production environments.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Skill is specifically focused on the operator-facing concerns that come up when connecting to and configuring Temporal Cloud — things like authentication, TLS, networking, and troubleshooting connection problems. This is distinct from the Developer Skill, which covers the programming model (workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, etc.). If you are struggling with why your Worker can't connect to Temporal Cloud, the Cloud Skill is the right tool to reach for.

questions.question: When you install a Temporal Skill into an AI coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor, what does that Skill primarily provide?

choices.text: A live connection to a running Temporal Cluster so the agent can start and cancel workflows on your behalf.
A set of pre-built Workflow templates the agent can copy into your project without requiring any customization.
Domain-specific Temporal knowledge — such as workflow determinism rules, retry policies, and testing strategies — that the agent can apply when helping you write code.
An automated testing harness that runs your Temporal Workflows inside the agent's sandbox environment before you deploy them.

questions.explanation: Skills give AI coding agents specialized expertise in a particular domain — in this case, Temporal's programming model. Without a Skill, a general-purpose AI agent might give generic or incorrect advice about Temporal concepts like determinism constraints, activity patterns, or versioning. With the Temporal Developer Skill installed, the agent has structured knowledge about these topics and can apply it to help you write correct Temporal code. Think of it like briefing a smart assistant on the specific rules of the system you're working with.

questions.question: A developer wants their AI coding agent to understand Temporal's workflow determinism rules, retry policies, and versioning gotchas while writing code. Which tool is specifically designed for this purpose?

choices.text: The Temporal Docs MCP Server, which streams live documentation into the agent's context window so it can answer questions about any Temporal concept in real time.
The Temporal Cloud Skill, which gives the agent expert knowledge of Temporal's programming model including workflow patterns and worker configuration.
The Temporal Developer Skill, which gives the agent expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model including determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, and versioning.
A custom system prompt injected into the agent that references the Temporal GitHub repository so the agent can browse source code and infer correct patterns.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill (github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer) is purpose-built to give AI coding agents domain-specific expertise in Temporal's programming model — including workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas. Option A describes the MCP Server, which provides real-time documentation access but is a different tool focused on answering questions rather than encoding deep programming-model expertise. Option B is wrong because the Temporal Cloud Skill is focused on troubleshooting Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues — not the programming model. Option D describes a manual workaround that Temporal does not recommend and that would not provide the structured expertise the Skill offers.

questions.question: What is the primary distinction between the Temporal Developer Skill and the Temporal Cloud Skill?

choices.text: The Developer Skill works only with Claude Code, while the Cloud Skill supports any MCP-compatible agent including Cursor and Codex.
The Developer Skill covers workflow programming patterns and SDK usage, while the Cloud Skill focuses on troubleshooting Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.
The Developer Skill is installed via the MCP server URL, while the Cloud Skill is installed via a plugin marketplace command specific to Claude Code.
The Developer Skill requires a Google account for authentication, while the Cloud Skill uses mTLS certificates tied to a Temporal Cloud namespace.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill is designed to give AI agents expertise in Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, testing strategies, and versioning. The Temporal Cloud Skill, by contrast, is focused on helping agents troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration problems. These are complementary but distinct concerns. Option A is wrong: both skills support multiple agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cline, Cursor, etc.) via the npx or manual install methods. Option C has the installation methods reversed; neither skill is installed via the MCP server URL — that URL is for the Temporal Docs MCP Server. Option D is wrong because the Google account authentication requirement applies to the Temporal Docs MCP Server (to enforce rate limits), not to either Skill.

questions.question:
A team wants AI assistants across all their projects to answer Temporal questions using current documentation rather than potentially stale training data. They configure the Temporal Docs MCP Server in Claude Code with this command:

```bash
claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
```

What does the `--scope user` flag achieve here?

choices.text: It registers the MCP server only for the current project and stores the configuration in the project's `.mcp.json` file so teammates can share it via version control.
It authenticates the MCP server connection using the current user's Temporal Cloud API key, bypassing the usual Google account login requirement.
It limits which Temporal documentation namespaces the MCP server can access, scoping responses to the user's registered Temporal Cloud account.
It registers the MCP server globally for the user so it is available across all projects, rather than being scoped to only the current project directory.

questions.explanation: The `--scope user` flag makes the MCP server registration global to the user's Claude Code installation, meaning it will be available in all projects. Without this flag, the configuration is stored in the project's `.mcp.json` file and is scoped only to that specific project. Option A describes the behavior when `--scope user` is omitted — the opposite of what the flag does. Option B is wrong; the MCP server uses anonymous authentication via any Google account to enforce rate limits, and this has nothing to do with Temporal Cloud API keys. Option C is a fabricated concept; the `--scope` flag controls project vs. global registration in Claude Code, not documentation access control.

questions.question: An engineer is configuring the Temporal Docs MCP Server in a tool that is not Claude Code or Claude Desktop. Which of the following correctly describes how to connect any MCP-compatible tool to the Temporal Docs MCP Server?

choices.text: Run `npx skills add https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer` and then set the transport to `stdio` in the tool's MCP configuration, since HTTP transport is only supported by Claude.
Use the server URL `https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai` with HTTP transport in the tool's MCP configuration, following that tool's specific JSON configuration format.
Clone the MCP server repository locally and point the tool to the local server process, because the hosted URL requires a Claude-specific authentication handshake.
Register the server using the `claude mcp add` CLI command regardless of which agent you are using, since this command writes a universal `.mcp.json` config file other tools also read.

questions.explanation: For any MCP-compatible tool, the Temporal Docs MCP Server is available at `https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai` using HTTP transport. The configuration format varies by tool, but the URL and transport type remain the same. The documentation provides a generic JSON snippet: `{"mcpServers":{"temporal-docs":{"transport":"http","url":"https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai"}}}`. Option A confuses the Docs MCP Server with the Developer Skill installation (npx skills add) and incorrectly claims HTTP transport is Claude-only — the server explicitly supports any MCP-compatible tool over HTTP. Option C is wrong; the hosted URL works for all tools and does not require a Claude-specific handshake — only a Google account login for rate limiting. Option D is wrong because `claude mcp add` is a Claude Code CLI command, not a universal tool, and it does not write a config file that other agents automatically consume.

questions.question: Your team uses an AI coding agent (Cursor) to generate Temporal workflow code. After deploying, you notice the agent frequently produces non-deterministic workflow code — for example, calling time.Now() directly inside workflow functions or spawning goroutines outside Temporal's SDK primitives. Which approach most directly addresses the root cause of this pattern?

choices.text: Switch to a different AI agent that has more recent training data, since newer models are less likely to produce non-deterministic code by default.
Add a post-generation linter that scans for known non-deterministic patterns and flags them before code is committed, relying on the agent's raw output otherwise.
Install the Temporal Developer Skill into the agent so it gains expert knowledge of workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, and common gotchas specific to Temporal's programming model.
Connect the agent to the Temporal Docs MCP Server so it can look up Temporal API reference pages on demand, replacing any static knowledge baked into its training weights.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill is explicitly designed to give AI coding agents 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas.' Non-deterministic code is the #1 source of workflow replay failures in production, and it happens because general-purpose LLMs have no inherent understanding of Temporal's execution model. The Skill embeds that domain knowledge so the agent avoids these pitfalls during generation, not just at lint time. The MCP Server (D) provides real-time doc lookup, which helps with API accuracy but does not embed the conceptual constraint that workflow code must be deterministic. A linter (B) catches symptoms after the fact and requires someone to maintain the rule set. Switching models (A) doesn't solve the underlying lack of Temporal-specific domain knowledge.

questions.question: You ask your Claude Code assistant to help you debug a Temporal Cloud connectivity issue — specifically, why your worker fails mTLS handshake against a newly provisioned namespace. The assistant confidently gives you an answer, but it references a certificate configuration option that was deprecated and removed six months ago. What is the most targeted fix to prevent this class of stale-knowledge error?

choices.text: Install the Temporal Developer Skill, which contains versioned documentation snapshots that are bundled at install time and therefore more current than the model's training data.
Add the Temporal Docs MCP Server to Claude Code so the assistant draws answers from live Temporal documentation rather than its training-data snapshot.
Install the Temporal Cloud Skill, which is specifically designed to help troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues using embedded expert knowledge.
Use the Temporal Cloud Skill together with the MCP Server simultaneously, because neither alone can resolve certificate-related questions without the other's context.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Docs MCP Server gives AI tools 'real-time access to our documentation, so responses draw from current docs rather than training data.' This directly addresses stale-knowledge errors where the model references removed or deprecated features — the server fetches current documentation at query time. The Temporal Cloud Skill (C) is valuable for Cloud-specific troubleshooting and would help with connectivity context, but it is still a static artifact cloned or installed at a point in time; it does not pull live docs. The Developer Skill (A) similarly is installed at a point in time. Option D overstates the dependency — the MCP Server alone is sufficient to get current documentation, though combining both tools is reasonable in practice.

questions.question: A platform team wants to standardize AI-assisted Temporal development across all their microservice repos. They need the Temporal Docs MCP Server available in every project without each developer having to configure it per-repo. Which Claude Code registration command achieves this?

choices.text: claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai — run inside each repo, which creates a .mcp.json committed to version control so the config is shared automatically.
claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai — run once per developer machine, making the server available globally across all projects for that user.
npx skills add https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai — run once, which registers the MCP server globally through the skills CLI rather than through Claude Code's own configuration system.
Clone the MCP server repository into ~/.claude/skills/temporal-docs, which causes Claude Code to auto-discover and register it as a global MCP server on next restart.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states: 'Add the Temporal docs MCP server globally so it's available in all your projects' using `claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai`. The `--scope user` flag is what makes it global — without it, the configuration goes into the project's `.mcp.json` file (option A), which only applies to that specific repo and must be repeated per project. Option C conflates the `npx skills` CLI (used for Skills, not MCP servers) with MCP server registration. Option D describes the manual installation path for Skills, not MCP servers — MCP servers are registered via `claude mcp add`, not by cloning into a skills directory.
